title: CODE OF HYGIENIC PRACTICE FOR EGGS AND EGG PRODUCTS CXC 15 - 1976

sections.section: CODE OF HYGIENIC PRACTICE FOR EGGS AND EGG PRODUCTS CXC 15 - 1976
sections.summary: Document title only: Code of Hygienic Practice for Eggs and Egg Products (CXC 15‑1976).
Table of contents only (non‑substantive), listing the Code's sections on objectives, scope, production, hygiene, transport, facilities, documentation and related topics.

Introduces the Code as guidance for safe production of eggs and egg products using a hazard‑analysis approach informed by FAO/WHO Salmonella risk assessments, focusing on domesticated chickens but applicable to other egg‑producing birds, and noting internal and external contamination sources and vulnerable consumer groups.

The Code's objective is to ensure the safety and suitability of eggs and egg products by applying the General Principles of Food Hygiene to all methods of primary production and processing, including guidance for small‑scale producers and processors.

sections.section: 2 SCOPE AND USE OF THE DOCUMENT
sections.summary: Scope: applies to primary production, sorting, grading, storage, transport, processing and distribution of shell eggs and egg products from domesticated birds for human consumption (excluding certain traditional delicacies); Use: supplements the General Principles of Food Hygiene, references other relevant Codex texts, allows flexibility for small‑scale/less‑developed producers, promotes hazard analysis/HACCP where practicable, emphasizes validated control measures and shared responsibilities across producers, processors and transporters, and provides specific definitions.

Applies to primary production, sorting, grading, storing, transport, processing and distribution of eggs in shell and egg products from domesticated birds intended for human consumption, excluding traditional delicacy eggs (e.g., Balut, 1000‑year‑old eggs).

States the Code is supplemental to the General Principles of Food Hygiene, references other relevant Codex standards, describes document formatting (principles/explanatory narratives/guidelines), defines applicability to safety and suitability, and affirms flexibility for different production systems—including small‑scale or less‑developed producers—with the use of 'where practicable' exemptions and referral to HACCP guidance for small businesses while still requiring adequate microbiological or other controls.

sections.section: 2.3 PRINCIPLES APPLYING TO THE PRODUCTION, HANDLING AND PROCESSING OF ALL EGGS AND EGG PRODUCTS
sections.summary: Sets principles requiring control measures from primary production through consumption to achieve appropriate public health protection, application of good hygienic/agricultural/manufacturing practices and HACCP where appropriate (recognizing limitations at primary production), and that controls should be effective and validated where practicable, with allowances for small/less‑developed businesses to follow national requirements or recognized guidelines when validation is not feasible.

sections.section: 2.4 RELATIVE ROLES OF EGG PRODUCERS, PROCESSORS AND TRANSPORTERS
sections.summary: Assigns shared responsibility across the egg production chain: producers must apply good hygienic, agricultural and animal‑husbandry practices; processors must implement GMP/GHP and adapt controls as needed; distributors, transporters, retailers and food preparers must handle and store eggs per instructions; and parties must maintain communication and provide consumer advice on safe handling.

Provides specific definitions for egg‑related terms used in the Code (supplementing the General Principles of Food Hygiene), including breaking, breeding flock, broken/leaker egg, candling, cracked egg, dirty egg, domesticated birds, egg laying establishment, egg product, incubator egg, microbiocidal treatment, pasteurization, shelf life and table egg.

Introduces primary production principles, noting flexibility for small or traditional holdings, and emphasizes that producers must take reasonable measures to minimize contamination during primary production because flock and environmental sources (including vertical/horizontal Salmonella transmission) critically affect egg safety and the controls required in subsequent processing.
States that the hygienic production provisions that follow apply equally to all egg producers.

Requires eggs to originate from healthy breeding and laying flocks and mandates good husbandry and biosecurity measures (e.g., parasite control, stress minimization, appropriate vaccines where permitted, flock monitoring—including for Salmonella Enteritidis—removal and safe disposal of dead/diseased birds), prudent veterinary drug use with records and adherence to withdrawal periods/MRLs, testing/sampling to verify controls, disposal or processing of eggs from infected flocks and, where practicable, culling of infected flocks and visitor hygiene controls.

sections.section: 3.2.2 Areas and Establishments for Egg Laying Systems
sections.summary: Requires egg‑laying areas and establishments to be designed, constructed, maintained and used to minimize hazards and pest exposure—covering appropriate internal design, cleaning and disinfection (preferably all‑in/all‑out where feasible), verification and corrective plans, litter, water, drainage and manure management, pest exclusion and minimizing waste accumulation.

Obliges producers to ensure feed does not introduce microbiological or chemical contaminants to flocks or eggs by employing good purchasing, handling, storage and processing practices (e.g., vendor assurances/certificates, avoiding mold/contamination, considering heat or other treatments) and keeping records of feed composition, origin, processing parameters and analyses.

Requires a properly designed pest control program to prevent pests as vectors of pathogens while ensuring pest control measures do not leave unacceptable residues in or on eggs, emphasizing prevention, pest‑proof feed storage, use of bait stations, approved chemical use per manufacturer instructions and safe storage of pesticides/rodenticides.

Mandates that procurement, transport, storage, use and disposal of agricultural and veterinary chemicals be managed to prevent contamination of eggs or flocks—used per manufacturer/competent authority instructions, kept in original labeled containers, applied by trained workers, recorded (date, product, concentration, purpose, location, withdrawal period), and with residues not exceeding Codex or national limits.

sections.section: ## 3.3  COLLECTION, HANDLING, STORAGE AND TRANSPORT OF EGGS
sections.summary: Requires eggs to be collected, handled, stored and transported to minimize contamination, shell damage and temperature fluctuations—minimizing time to controlled storage, segregating or diverting cracked/dirty/broken/incubator eggs from the table egg trade to processing or safe disposal, and segregating species/production systems as appropriate, with processors communicating farm‑level requirements.
